communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Georgia Open Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

The most recent records retention schedule for the agency, which includes a list of documents kept by the agency and for how long each record is retained. This may be called a general records schedule, a records index, or something else. Please release this in a PDF (.pdf) if possible.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 3 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Curt Merrill

title: Obama Presidential Library: Saudi Investment in Twitter Inc. and National Security Implications (2011–2017)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Aaccess to and copies of all records, including but not limited to memoranda, email correspondence, PowerPoint presentations, audio recordings, video recordings, briefing papers, and situation reports, created or received by the Executive Office of the President (EOP) between January 1, 2011, and January 20, 2017, regarding the ownership of Twitter Inc. by Saudi nationals and entities.

Scope of Request:
I am specifically seeking records from the National Security Council (NSC), the National Economic Council (NEC), and the Office of the Vice President that discuss:

The 2011 initial investment and the October 2015 increase in shareholding (to approximately 5.2%) in Twitter Inc. by Prince Alwaleed bin Talal and/or the Kingdom Holding Company (KHC).

Any inter-agency assessments regarding the national security implications of Saudi "passive" investment in U.S. social media platforms, specifically concerning the protection of U.S. person data and the potential for foreign surveillance.

Communications or briefings regarding the FBI’s 2015 notification to Twitter Inc. regarding the infiltration of the company by agents acting on behalf of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (e.g., the Ali Alzabarah and Ahmad Abouammo cases).

Records discussing the transition of influence or assets from Prince Alwaleed bin Talal to Mohammad bin Salman (MBS) or the Public Investment Fund (PIF) as it relates to U.S. technology firms.

Statutory Acknowledgment regarding Section 721(c):

I recognize that Section 721(c) of the Defense Production Act (50 U.S.C. § 4565) provides a statutory exemption for information filed with CFIUS. However, this request specifically excludes the raw 'notice' or 'declaration' filed by the private parties. Instead, it seeks the derivative executive branch records—such as policy memoranda, NSC/NEC briefing slides, and inter-agency correspondence—that discuss the national security implications of the social media and telecommunications industry and the impact of Saudi sovereign investment on U.S. digital infrastructure. I request that all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions of these records be released.

Please provide any reasonable segregable portion of a requested record after removing or redacting those portions claimed to be exempt, explain in writing the justification for redacting the record, indicate the extent of any redactions on the portion of the record which is made available or published and where technically feasible indicate on the redacted portion itself the specific exemption(s) claimed.

Search Keywords:

"Twitter", "Alwaleed bin Talal", "Kingdom Holding Company", "KHC", "Mohammad bin Salman", "MBS", "Public Investment Fund", "PIF", "CFIUS", "Spy Ring", "Alzabarah", "Abouammo", "Saudi Surveillance", "Data Privacy", "Social Media Foreign Ownership".

Thank you to the Barack Obama Presidential Library and for the library's commitment to transparency.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware

title: Obama Presidential Library: CFIUS Review of Aditya Birla Group / Columbian Chemicals (2010–2012)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Access to and copies of all records created or received by the Executive Office of the President (EOP) between January 1, 2010, and December 31, 2012, regarding the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS) national security review of the acquisition of Columbian Chemicals by the Aditya Birla Group (including its subsidiaries Indigold Carbon, Alexandria Carbon Black, or Thai Carbon Black).

For additional context:

https://www.k-online.com/en/Media_News/News/K_Archive/Birla_Carbon_to_acquire_Columbian_Chemicals

Scope of Request:

This is a request for the government's "derivative work" related to a CFIUS review of the Columbian Chemical acquisition by Aditya Birla. I am seeking high-level briefings, memoranda, situation room reports, PowerPoint Presentations, audio and/or video recordings and email correspondence within the National Security Council (NSC), the National Economic Council (NEC), and the Office of the Vice President that discuss:

The strategic importance of the carbon black supply chain to the U.S. automotive and defense industries.

Potential national security risks associated with the transfer of Columbian Chemicals’ proprietary R&D and manufacturing assets to a foreign conglomerate.

The "Industrial Security" implications of the 2011 acquisition, specifically any concerns regarding the conglomerate's global footprint or ties to other foreign powers.

For the purposes of redaction: I recognize that Section 721(c) of the Defense Production Act (50 U.S.C. § 4565) provides a statutory exemption for information filed with CFIUS. However, this request specifically excludes the raw 'notice' or 'declaration' filed by the private parties. Instead, it seeks the derivative executive branch records—such as policy memoranda, NSC briefing slides, and inter-agency correspondence—that discuss the national security implications of the carbon black industry and the Aditya Birla Group’s entry into the U.S. market. I request that all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions of these records be released. In plain language this request asks for line-by-line evidence of what is being redacted. 

Search Keywords:

To facilitate your search, please include the following keywords in your query of the Electronic Records Archive (ERA):

"Columbian Chemicals", "Aditya Birla", "Birla Carbon", "Indigold Carbon", "CFIUS", "Carbon Black", "Kumar Mangalam Birla", "One Equity Partners", "JPMorgan Chase", "Centerville LA", "Marietta GA", "Dr. Santrupt Misra"

Thank you to the Obama Library's commitment to transparency. I look forward to working with you in resolving this request.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware
